> Every guide, in markdown, for AI ingestion.

# Are Peptide Reviews Online Trustworthy? (Why So Many Are Paid)

_A lot of peptide 'reviews' are paid placements or vendor-seeded. Here's how to tell a real review from a planted one, and what to trust instead. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/are-peptide-reviews-trustworthy.html · Updated: July 7, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer: treat most of them as marketing.** A large share of peptide "reviews" — blog round-ups, "best vendor" lists, glowing forum threads — are paid placements, affiliate content, or vendor-seeded. That doesn't make them all worthless, but it means a review is a *starting point*, never proof. Verifiable testing is the only thing that proves anything.

## Why the reviews are so often compromised

- **Affiliate incentive:** many "best peptide vendor" lists earn a commission on every sale — the ranking follows the payout, not the quality.

- **Vendor-seeded praise:** a fresh forum account appearing only to praise one vendor, or a wave of five-star reviews in a short window, is a classic plant.

- **Paid placements:** some "review" sites simply sell the top spot.

## How to tell a real review from a planted one

- **Does it criticize anything?** Real experience is mixed. All-glow, no-flaws reads like copy.

- **Is the account established?** A reviewer with history across many topics beats a one-post account that only ever praises a single store.

- **Is there a disclosed affiliate relationship?** Disclosure is actually a *good* sign — it's honest. Hidden affiliate links are the red flag.

- **Does it point to verifiable testing,** or just vibes? A useful review shows you *how* to check, not just "trust me."

## What to trust instead

Skip the popularity contest and check the thing that can't be bought: independent, verifiable lab testing. Does the COA resolve on the [lab's own portal](/articles/how-to-verify-a-coa.html)? Is the lab [independent or captive](/articles/what-is-a-captive-lab.html)? Is there [any blind testing](/articles/coa-reuse-and-theft.html)? That's signal; a five-star banner is noise.

[🧪 Skip the reviews — score a vendor's testing in 60 seconds →](/vendor-quiz.html)
Related guides[How to read a peptide COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html) · [How to spot a fake testing lab](/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html) · [Buying peptides in Canada](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html) · [The Growth Guys discount code](/articles/growth-guys-discount-code.html)

# Are Peptides Legal in Canada? What the Rules Actually Say (2026)

_A plain, neutral explainer of how peptides are regulated in Canada in 2026 — research chemicals vs. approved drugs, the Health Canada position, and what it means for buyers. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/are-peptides-legal-in-canada.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer:** It's a grey area. Most research peptides are **not approved by Health Canada for human use**, and selling them *for human consumption* is not permitted. They are widely sold and bought as **"research chemicals" for laboratory and educational purposes only**. This page explains the distinction — it is general information, not legal advice.

## The key distinction: approved drug vs. research chemical

In Canada, a substance marketed to *diagnose, treat, or affect the body* is regulated as a drug and needs Health Canada authorization (a DIN). Most peptides sold online do **not** have that authorization. Vendors therefore sell them labelled strictly as **research chemicals "not for human consumption."** That label is the legal line they operate behind.

## What that means in practice

- **Selling for human use** — not permitted without authorization. Vendors who imply human use take on real risk.

- **Selling as research material** — the grey-area model most vendors use.

- **Importing** — shipments can be examined, delayed, or seized at the border, which is a major reason buyers prefer [domestic Canadian vendors](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html).

⚠️ **Health Canada has issued advisories** about unauthorized peptide products, and regulators have signalled increased attention to how they're marketed (including by online sellers and affiliates). Treat any product claim with skepticism, and understand you're buying unapproved material.

## Why this makes vendor diligence even more important

Because these products sit outside the approved-drug system, **no regulator is checking what's actually in the vial.** That responsibility falls entirely on you. The only real signal of quality is independent, third-party lab testing that you can verify yourself — which is why learning to [read a COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html) matters more here than in almost any other purchase.

[🧪 Check a vendor's testing in 60 seconds →](/vendor-quiz.html)

Related guides[How to read a peptide COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html) · [How to spot a fake testing lab](/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html) · [Buying peptides in Canada](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html) · [The Growth Guys discount code](/articles/growth-guys-discount-code.html)

# The Safest Way to Buy Peptides in Canada (2026 Guide)

_A practical, step-by-step way to source peptides in Canada with the least risk — domestic shipping, verifiable testing, and a vendor shortlist. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/buy-peptides-canada-safely.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **The safest path in one line:** buy from a **domestic Canadian vendor** (no border risk) whose testing is done by an **independent lab and verifies on the lab's own portal**, and treat everything as **research/educational material** — which is its legal status in Canada.

## Step 1 — Stay domestic

Overseas orders can be delayed, held, or seized at customs. A Canadian vendor shipping domestically removes that risk entirely and gets your order to you faster. This single choice eliminates the most common practical headache. (More on the legal status: [are peptides legal in Canada?](/articles/are-peptides-legal-in-canada.html))

## Step 2 — Demand verifiable testing

No regulator checks what's in these vials, so testing is your only quality signal — and not all "testing" counts. Require:

- A COA that **verifies on the lab's own portal** (not just a vendor PDF).

- An **independent lab** used by many vendors (not a captive one spun up for a single brand).

- Bonus: any **independent blind testing** of the actual product.

## Step 3 — Score the vendor before you pay

## Step 4 — Keep a shortlist

Don't marry one vendor. Keep two or three that ship domestically and pass the testing checks, and buy from whoever has what you need in stock. See [buying peptides in Canada](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html) for the longer version.

# Buying Peptides in Canada (2026): A Practical Sourcing Guide

_How to source peptides in Canada in 2026 — why domestic shipping matters, how to vet a vendor's testing, and what to check before you buy. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **How do you buy peptides in Canada?** Buy from a **domestic (Canadian) vendor** to avoid customs delays and seizures, and choose one whose purity is confirmed by **independent, third-party lab testing** — not just a PDF the vendor uploaded. Peptides here are sold for **research and educational purposes only**; they are not approved for human use.

## 1. Why domestic shipping matters most

The single biggest practical headache when sourcing in Canada is the border. Orders shipped from overseas can be delayed, held, or seized at customs — which costs you money and time. A Canadian vendor shipping domestically sidesteps that entirely: faster delivery, no border risk, and a real address you can look up. If two vendors look equal, the one shipping inside Canada wins on practicality alone.

## 2. Testing beats price — every time

A cheap vial of the wrong thing isn't a deal. The peptide market has a documented trust problem: a Certificate of Analysis (COA) only proves a test was done on *some* sample — not that it came from the vial you'll receive. Before you compare prices, compare *testing transparency*:

- Does the COA verify on the **lab's own portal**, not just a vendor PDF?

- Is the lab **independent** (used by many competing vendors), or captive (only this one)?

- Is there any **independent blind / community testing** — the gold standard?

We break this down fully in [how to read a peptide COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html).

## 3. Vet any vendor in 60 seconds

Rather than guess, run the vendor through a structured check. Our free quiz scores exactly the signals above and flags the red flags for you.

[🧪 Take the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)

## 4. The shortlist approach

Smart Canadian buyers keep a short list of two or three domestic vendors that (a) ship inside Canada, (b) use independent testing that verifies on the lab's own site, and (c) are open and specific when asked about their testing. Then they buy from whichever has what they need in stock.

## A verified option

If you want a starting point, the vendor we point to uses genuine independent third-party testing, and there's a standing discount.

> **Code:** HEALTHYLIFE10 — 10% off · [see the vendor →](https://growthguys.netlify.app)

# How to Evaluate a Canadian Peptide Vendor (2026)

_A practical framework for judging Canadian peptide vendors in 2026 — domestic shipping, verifiable independent testing, and transparency. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/canadian-peptide-vendors.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **The framework in one line:** a Canadian vendor worth using ships **domestically** (no border risk), backs its products with **independent testing you can verify yourself**, and answers testing questions **openly**. Everything else is secondary. Research/educational use only.

## 1. Domestic shipping

This is the Canada-specific filter. A vendor shipping from inside Canada avoids customs delays and seizures — the most common practical problem with overseas orders. Confirm a real Canadian address and domestic fulfilment before anything else.

## 2. Verifiable independent testing

No regulator checks these products, so testing is your only quality signal. But "testing" only counts if it's independent and verifiable. Ask: does the Certificate of Analysis resolve on the *lab's own* portal (not just a vendor PDF)? Is the lab used by many competing vendors, or only this one? Full method: [how to read a COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html).

## 3. Transparency under questioning

Email and ask how they test. Specific, open answers and a portal link are good signs. Vague "trust us" replies or "ISO-aligned" weasel wording are not.

## 4. A findable, consistent reputation

Look for a consistent presence across independent communities over time — not a brand that appeared last month with only its own glowing words.

## Score any vendor in 60 seconds

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)

# Purity vs Identity on a COA: Don't Confuse the Two

_A COA reports identity and purity separately, and buyers mix them up. What each means and why a high purity number alone can mislead. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/coa-purity-vs-identity.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer:** A COA reports two separate things people constantly confuse. **Identity** = is it the right compound. **Purity** = what percentage of the sample is that compound. A 99% pure sample of the *wrong* molecule is still useless.

## Identity, explained

Identity testing confirms the sample is the molecule named on the label. Without it, a purity number is floating free — pure *what*, exactly?

## Purity, explained

Purity is the share of the sample that's the target compound, with the rest being by-products, fragments, or filler. A higher number is better, but only once identity is confirmed.

## The trap of reading one number

Marketing loves a big purity figure because it sounds impressive in isolation. Always pair it with the identity result before you trust it.

Related: [HPLC vs mass spec](/articles/hplc-vs-mass-spec-peptide-testing.html) · [how to read a COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html).

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)

# COA Reuse and Theft, Explained (2026)

_The two ways a 'real' Certificate of Analysis still misleads you: reuse (one test, thousands of vials) and theft (someone else's COA). Here's how each works._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/coa-reuse-and-theft.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **The core idea:** a Certificate of Analysis proves a test happened on *some* sample — not that it came from your vial, nor that the vendor showing it is the one who ordered it. That gap is exploited two ways: **reuse** and **theft**. A "real" PDF defeats neither.

## Reuse

A supplier tests one good batch and gets a genuine COA. Then thousands of vials — many never tested — are sold against that single report. Every reseller buying from that supplier can show the same document, even though your specific vial was never analyzed. One test, an ocean of untested product riding on it.

## Theft

A vendor downloads someone else's genuine COA — a competitor's, or their supplier's — and presents it as proof of *their* product. The document is authentic; it's just not theirs. Posting a real PDF only defeats forgery. It does nothing against a stolen-but-real report.

## Why "the PDF is real" isn't enough

People check whether a COA looks legitimate and stop there. But authenticity is the floor, not the ceiling. The real questions are: whose sample was tested, and does the result tie to *this* vendor and *this* batch?

## How to defeat both

- Insist the COA **verifies on the lab's own portal**, with the vendor named as the client.

- Prefer **independent blind testing** — where a buyer purchases off-the-shelf and submits it, so the vendor can't choose the sample.

Related: [why two vendors share a COA](/articles/why-two-vendors-same-coa.html) · [how to read a COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html).

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)
Related guides[How to read a peptide COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html) · [How to spot a fake testing lab](/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html) · [Buying peptides in Canada](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html) · [The Growth Guys discount code](/articles/growth-guys-discount-code.html)

# How Much Does It Cost to Test Your Own Peptide Vial?

_What independent identity and purity testing of a peptide vial typically costs, and why the low price makes skipping it a red flag. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/cost-to-test-a-peptide-vial.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer:** Independent identity and purity testing of a single sample is a modest flat fee at third-party labs — cheap relative to the order it protects. The number itself carries a lesson: if a buyer can afford to test, a vendor selling thousands of vials certainly can.

## What you're paying for

A typical test bundle covers identity (is this the molecule it claims to be?) and purity (how much of the sample is that molecule?). Some buyers also pay for quantification. It's a small, predictable cost per sample.

## Why "too expensive to test" is a red flag

When testing is this affordable, a vendor that skips it isn't saving money — it's avoiding scrutiny. The cost excuse doesn't hold up against the math of selling at scale.

## How to submit a sample

- Pick an **independent** lab, not one affiliated with the vendor.

- Buy the product like a normal customer (a blind purchase).

- Compare the result against the vendor's own published COA.

Related: [blind testing vs vendor COA](/articles/independent-blind-testing-vs-vendor-coa.html).

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)

# Do Peptide Companies Test Every Batch? (The Honest Answer)

_Most peptide vendors do NOT test every batch — they test once and reuse the COA. Here's how batch testing really works and how to tell if your specific vial was tested._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/do-vendors-test-every-batch.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer: most don't.** Genuine per-batch testing is expensive, so many vendors test once (or rely on their supplier's single test) and show that **same COA** for everything after. Unless you can tie the certificate to *your* batch, "lab tested" often means "a batch was tested once," not "this vial was tested."

## How batch testing actually works

A "batch" (or lot) is one production run. True per-batch testing means each lot gets its own fresh analysis with its own COA and lot number. That's the gold standard — and it's costly, which is exactly why it's rare. The cheaper, common shortcut is [COA reuse](/articles/coa-reuse-and-theft.html): one good test stretched across many untested lots.

## How to tell if YOUR vial was tested

- **Lot number match:** does the lot on your physical vial match the lot on the COA? (Many vendors don't even print one.)

- **Recent, specific date:** a per-batch COA is recent and tied to that run — not a single document dated long ago and reused.

- **Portal verification:** the result resolves on the [lab's own portal](/articles/how-to-verify-a-coa.html), tied to that lot.

- **Independent blind testing:** the strongest proof — someone bought off-the-shelf and tested it, so the vendor couldn't cherry-pick the sample.

## The takeaway

"Do they test every batch?" is the right question to ask a vendor directly. An open, specific answer (with per-lot COAs you can verify) is a strong sign; a vague "everything is lab tested" usually means one test doing a lot of work.

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)
Related guides[How to read a peptide COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html) · [How to spot a fake testing lab](/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html) · [Buying peptides in Canada](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html) · [The Growth Guys discount code](/articles/growth-guys-discount-code.html)

# Does a Peptide COA Expire? What the Date Really Means

_A Certificate of Analysis is a snapshot of one batch on one date. What the date means, why batch-matching beats freshness, and what to ask for. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/does-a-peptide-coa-expire.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer:** A COA is a snapshot of one batch tested on one date. It doesn't "expire" like milk — but an old COA tied to a batch you're not actually buying tells you little about the vial in your cart. Batch-matching matters more than the calendar.

## What the date actually means

The date tells you when that specific batch was tested. It's useful context, but a recent date on the wrong batch is no more relevant than an old date on the right one.

## Batch-matching beats freshness

The question that matters: does this COA correspond to the *batch you're receiving*? A perfectly fresh report for a different lot doesn't describe your product.

## What to ask for

- A COA whose **batch or lot number matches** what you're buying.

- Verification on the **lab's own portal**, with the vendor named.

Related: [COA reuse and theft](/articles/coa-reuse-and-theft.html) · [how to verify a COA](/articles/how-to-verify-a-coa.html).

# Domestic vs Overseas Peptide Vendors: What Actually Differs

_For a Canadian buyer, what genuinely differs between domestic and overseas peptide vendors — shipping, the border, and recourse — and what doesn't. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/domestic-vs-overseas-peptide-vendors.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer:** For a Canadian buyer the practical differences are shipping time, the customs question, and your recourse if an order goes wrong. A domestic vendor simplifies all three — but none of that tells you anything about quality. Only testing does.

## Shipping and the border

A domestic order avoids the international-parcel uncertainty entirely. Faster delivery, no border question, fewer unknowns. That's a convenience difference, not a quality one.

## Recourse and accountability

If something's wrong with a domestic order, you generally have a clearer path to sort it out than with a parcel shipped from overseas. Accountability is easier to pursue closer to home.

## Why neither replaces testing

A smooth, fast domestic delivery of an untested vial is still an untested vial. Sourcing convenience and product quality are two separate questions — don't let one stand in for the other.

Related: [peptides and Canadian customs](/articles/peptides-canada-customs.html) · [buying peptides in Canada](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html).

## Why the vendor matters more than the % off

A discount is only a deal if the product behind it is real. Before chasing any code, check the vendor's *testing*: does the Certificate of Analysis verify on the lab's own portal? Is the lab independent (used by many vendors) or captive (only this one)? Is there independent blind testing? That's why this code points to a vendor using genuine third-party testing — the discount is the cherry on top.

[Check any vendor in 60 seconds →](/vendor-quiz.html)

> **Short answer:** Comparing Canadian peptide vendors comes down to three things you can actually verify — independent testing, domestic shipping, and a track record — not the marketing on the homepage.

## Testing is the only one that matters

Everything else is convenience. A vendor that shows independent, third-party results that verify on the lab's own portal, with the vendor named as the client, has cleared the bar most never reach.

## Shipping and recourse

Domestic shipping means no border uncertainty and a clearer path if an order goes wrong. It's a convenience difference, not a quality one.

## Track record and transparency

How long they've operated, whether they answer a direct testing question plainly, and whether their COAs match the batch you receive. Evasiveness is its own answer.

# How to Read a Peptide COA (And Spot a Fake One)

_A Certificate of Analysis proves a test happened on some sample — not that it came from your vial. Here's the trust hierarchy and a 6-point checklist before you buy._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **What is a peptide COA?** A Certificate of Analysis is a lab document reporting what a tested sample contained. The key thing to understand: **a COA proves a test was performed on *some* sample — not that it came from the vial you'll receive, nor that the company showing it is the one who paid for the test.**

## The core lesson most buyers miss

A "real" COA only defeats *forgery*. It does nothing against the two attacks that actually happen:

- **Reuse** — one good vial gets tested, and thousands of vials are sold against that single COA.

- **Theft** — a vendor posts someone else's genuine COA (a competitor's, or their supplier's) as their own.

Even the founder of one of the most respected testing labs has said publicly that if someone wanted to send a misleading sample, the lab has no way to verify where it came from. The document being authentic is the floor, not the ceiling.

## The trust hierarchy — weakest to strongest

- **A COA PDF on the vendor's own site** → weakest. Could be reused, stolen, or not your batch.

- **The COA resolves on the *lab's own* verification portal** → defeats forgery.

- **Client name on the COA matches the vendor + the lot on your vial matches the COA** → a provenance link.

- **Independent blind / community testing** → the gold standard; the vendor can't pick the sample.

- **You test your own vial yourself** at an independent lab → ultimate certainty.

## How to spot a fake or captive "lab"

Some vendors spin up a "lab" that exists to test only for them. Tells to watch for (a pattern, not pointed at any one company):

- The "lab's" website was created right around the same time as the vendor's — sometimes days apart.

- It has no clients other than that one vendor; no reviews, accreditation, or staff footprint.

- Weasel wording like "ISO 17025-aligned" — which is not the same as accredited.

- A "verify" portal that returns OK for any number you type in, even a made-up one.

- A signatory "chemist" who appears only as a signature line and matches no real record.

## Your 6-point COA checklist

- Does the COA verify on the lab's own portal, not just a vendor PDF?

- Is the lab used by many competing vendors (independent), not just this one (captive)?

- Is the lab established, not spun up alongside the vendor?

- Is there any independent blind/community testing?

- Does the client name match the vendor — and can you match your vial's lot to the COA?

- Is the vendor open and specific about testing when you ask, or vague?

Want this scored for you automatically?

[🧪 Take the free 60-second vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)

## The bottom line

Reading a COA isn't about trusting a PDF — it's about asking *whose* sample, verified *where*, by a lab accountable to *whom*. Get those three answers and you've done more diligence than most buyers ever will.

Related guides[How to spot a fake testing lab](/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html) · [Buying peptides in Canada](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html) · [The Growth Guys discount code](/articles/growth-guys-discount-code.html) · [Free vendor-check quiz](/vendor-quiz.html)

# How to Spot a Fake Peptide Testing Lab (Captive-Lab Red Flags)

_Some vendors run a 'lab' that exists only to test their own products. Here are the captive-lab red flags — domain age, single client, fake verify portals — so you can tell real testing from theatre._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **The tell in one line:** a "captive" lab exists to test for *one* vendor only — that's grading your own homework. A real independent lab tests for **many competing vendors**. The red flags below help you spot the difference. (These are general patterns, not an accusation about any specific company.)

Smart vendors know buyers now ask for lab results — so some manufacture the *appearance* of independent testing instead of the real thing. Here's how that theatre usually looks.

**🚩 1. The "lab" appeared right after the vendor.** Check when each website was created (a free WHOIS lookup shows this). A genuine lab is usually established and older. A captive shell often has a domain registered days or weeks after the vendor it "independently" tests for.

**🚩 2. It has exactly one client.** Search the lab's name. A real, independent lab is referenced by many different vendors and community members. A captive one appears *only* alongside the single vendor it serves — no other clients, no reviews, no footprint.

**🚩 3. "Verify" that verifies anything.** Type a made-up, random report number into the lab's verification portal. If it returns a valid-looking "OK" for nonsense, the portal is theatre. A real portal returns a specific record for real numbers and nothing for fake ones.

**🚩 4. Weasel accreditation wording.** Phrases like "ISO 17025-aligned" or "ISO-standard methods" are *not* the same as being accredited. Real accreditation is a specific, checkable status — not an adjective.

**🚩 5. A phantom signatory.** The "chemist" who signs the COA appears only as a signature line across that vendor's documents and matches no real professional record anywhere. Real people who run labs have a footprint.

**🚩 6. No independent path at all.** The strongest signal isn't on the COA — it's whether anyone has done *blind* testing (buying off the shelf and submitting it themselves). If the only testing in existence is the one the vendor controls, treat it as marketing.

## The reliable move

You don't need to become a detective on every purchase. Run the vendor through a structured check that scores exactly these signals — it takes about a minute.

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)

Want the deeper version? Read [how to read a peptide COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html).

Related guides[How to read a peptide COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html) · [Buying peptides in Canada](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html) · [The Growth Guys discount code](/articles/growth-guys-discount-code.html) · [Free vendor-check quiz](/vendor-quiz.html)

# How to Verify a Peptide COA on the Lab's Own Portal (2026)

_A vendor-hosted PDF proves little. Here's the step-by-step way to verify a Certificate of Analysis on the testing lab's own portal — and what a pass vs. fail looks like._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/how-to-verify-a-coa.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **The one rule:** don't trust a PDF the vendor hands you — confirm the result on the **testing lab's own website**. A real lab lets you look up a report independently; a fake one can't. Here's how to do it in under two minutes.

## Step by step

- **Find the lab name + report/verification number** on the COA (usually near the header or footer).

- **Go to the lab's own website directly** — type it in yourself, don't use a link on the vendor's store (a fake "portal" can be hosted by the vendor).

- **Use the lab's lookup/verify tool** and enter the report number.

- **Check what comes back:** a genuine portal returns a specific, server-rendered record that matches the PDF — same compound, same purity, same date.

## The fake-portal test

Before you trust any "verify" page, type in a **made-up, random number**. A real portal returns nothing (or an error). A fake one returns a valid-looking "OK" for anything — which means it's theatre, not verification.

## Pass vs. fail

- ✅ **Pass:** the report resolves on the lab's own domain, matches the PDF, and names the vendor as the client.

- ❌ **Fail:** no portal, a portal on the vendor's own site, a portal that accepts fake numbers, or a record that doesn't match the PDF.

Background: [how to read a COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html) · [what is a captive lab](/articles/what-is-a-captive-lab.html).

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)
Related guides[How to read a peptide COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html) · [How to spot a fake testing lab](/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html) · [Buying peptides in Canada](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html) · [The Growth Guys discount code](/articles/growth-guys-discount-code.html)

_General educational information, not claims about any specific company. For research and educational purposes only. Not medical advice. Posts may contain affiliate links._


---

# HPLC vs Mass Spec: What Each Peptide Test Actually Tells You

_Mass spectrometry confirms identity; HPLC measures purity. What each peptide test proves, and why one without the other is incomplete. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/hplc-vs-mass-spec-peptide-testing.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **The core idea:** HPLC and mass spectrometry answer different questions. Mass spec confirms *identity* — is this the molecule it claims to be? HPLC measures *purity* — how much of the sample is that molecule versus everything else? A complete COA needs both.

## What mass spec proves

Mass spectrometry weighs the molecule and matches it to the expected mass. It answers "is this the right compound?" — but on its own it says little about how much filler or by-product rides along.

## What HPLC proves

HPLC separates a sample into its components and reports the percentage that is the target compound. It answers "how clean is it?" — but on its own it doesn't confirm the main peak is the right molecule.

## Why one alone isn't enough

A high HPLC purity number on the wrong molecule is meaningless, and a confirmed identity with unknown purity is half a picture. Read both, together.

Related: [purity vs identity on a COA](/articles/coa-purity-vs-identity.html) · [how to read a COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html).

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)

_General educational information, not claims about any specific company. For research and educational purposes only. Not medical advice. Posts may contain affiliate links._

# Importing Peptides From China to Canada: Why the Math Rarely Works

_Ordering peptides direct from China to Canada looks cheap until you add customs risk, no recourse, and unverifiable testing. Here's the real math and the safer alternative._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/importing-peptides-china-canada.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **The headline price is not the real price.** Ordering direct from an overseas supplier looks cheapest per vial — until you price in customs seizures, zero recourse, and testing you can't verify. Once those are in the math, a domestic Canadian vendor with real independent testing usually wins on *expected* cost, not just peace of mind.

## The hidden costs the per-vial price hides

- **Customs risk.** A held or seized shipment is a 100% loss — and there's no refund and no one to appeal to. Factor the probability of loss into every order and the "cheap" price climbs fast.

- **No recourse.** Wrong product, short fill, or nothing arrives? With an anonymous overseas seller, you have no realistic way to make it right.

- **Unverifiable testing.** The COA you're shown is often the supplier's own — the same document passed around dozens of resellers ([COA reuse](/articles/coa-reuse-and-theft.html)). You can't tie it to your vial.

- **Time + hassle.** Long transit, tracking gaps, and the stress of wondering if it'll clear.

## The expected-cost way to think about it

Don't compare sticker prices — compare *expected* cost: price ÷ the chance it actually arrives as described. A slightly pricier domestic vial that reliably shows up, with testing you can verify on the [lab's own portal](/articles/how-to-verify-a-coa.html), beats a cheap one with a real chance of arriving as nothing.

## The safer Canadian alternative

A vendor shipping [domestically within Canada](/articles/buy-peptides-canada-safely.html) with independent testing removes the border lottery entirely. See [how to evaluate a Canadian vendor](/articles/canadian-peptide-vendors.html), or score one instantly:

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)

# Independent Blind Testing vs a Vendor's COA: The Real Gold Standard

_A vendor COA tests a sample the vendor picked. Independent blind testing removes that variable. Here's why it's the gold standard. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/independent-blind-testing-vs-vendor-coa.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer:** A vendor COA shows a test the vendor chose to run on a sample the vendor chose to send. Independent blind testing means a buyer purchases off-the-shelf, unannounced, and submits it — so the vendor can't hand-pick the vial. That single difference removes what makes a COA gameable.

## The sample-selection problem

The weakness of any vendor-supplied COA isn't the lab — it's who chose the sample. If the seller picks which vial gets tested, a clean result only proves that one vial was clean. Blind testing closes that loophole.

## What blind-testing programs do

Community blind-testing efforts buy product like a normal customer would and submit it to an independent lab. Because the vendor doesn't know which order is being tested, there's no way to send a "good" vial. The result reflects what a real buyer actually receives.

## How to use this when buying

- Prefer vendors whose product has appeared in **independent blind tests**, not just self-published COAs.

- A vendor COA is the floor; blind-test results are the ceiling.

Related: [COA reuse and theft](/articles/coa-reuse-and-theft.html) · [how to read a COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html).

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)

# What Does ISO 17025 Mean for a Peptide Lab?

_ISO 17025 is the accreditation standard for testing labs. Here's what it actually means, why 'ISO 17025-aligned' is a red flag, and how to check if a lab is really accredited._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/iso-17025-peptide-lab.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer:** ISO/IEC 17025 is the international standard for the **competence of testing and calibration laboratories**. A lab that is **accredited** to it has been independently audited to prove its methods and results are reliable. The catch: **"ISO 17025-aligned" or "ISO-standard methods" is NOT accreditation** — it's weasel wording that borrows the credibility without earning it.

## Accredited vs. "aligned"

- **Accredited** = a recognized accreditation body audited the lab and issued a certificate with a scope. It's a checkable, specific status.

- **"Aligned" / "follows ISO 17025 methods"** = a marketing phrase any lab can write about itself, with no audit and no certificate behind it.

If a "lab" leans on the second phrasing, treat the accreditation claim as decoration.

## How to check (free)

- **Ask for the accreditation certificate + the accrediting body's name.** Real accreditation is documented.

- **Look it up on the accreditation body's public register.** Accredited labs are listed publicly; "aligned" ones won't be.

- **Check the scope.** Accreditation covers specific methods — make sure it actually covers the testing they're selling (e.g. HPLC/LC-MS purity), not something unrelated.

## The bigger picture

Accreditation is one signal — but for peptides, **independence matters even more than the badge.** A captive lab could in theory be accredited and still be grading its own homework. Combine the accreditation check with the [captive-lab test](/articles/what-is-a-captive-lab.html) and a result that [verifies on the lab's own portal](/articles/how-to-verify-a-coa.html).

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)

---
_General educational information, not claims about any specific company. For research and educational purposes only. Not medical advice. Posts may contain affiliate links._


---

# Janoshik vs In-House Testing: Why the Difference Matters

_Independent third-party testing versus a vendor's own in-house numbers — what the difference means when you're vetting a peptide source. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/janoshik-vs-in-house-testing.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **The core idea:** An independent lab and a vendor's own in-house testing are not the same thing. Independent testing — for example a widely used third-party lab like Janoshik — means a separate party ran the analysis. In-house means the seller graded its own homework.

## What "independent" actually means

An independent result comes from a lab with no stake in the outcome. The value isn't the PDF itself — it's that the party running the test doesn't profit from a passing grade. That separation is the whole point.

## Why in-house numbers deserve more scrutiny

When a seller tests its own product and publishes only the results it likes, you're seeing a curated picture, not an audit. This isn't an accusation against any specific company — it's a structural problem with self-reporting anywhere.

## What to ask for

- A COA that **verifies on the testing lab's own portal**.

- The vendor **named as the client** on that report.

- Ideally, **independent blind testing** — a buyer purchases off-the-shelf and submits it, so the sample can't be hand-picked.

Related: [COA reuse and theft](/articles/coa-reuse-and-theft.html) · [how to spot a fake testing lab](/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html).

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)

# COA Batch Numbers: Why They Have to Match Your Vial

_Why a peptide COA's batch number must match the number on your vial, where to find it, and what to do if they don't match. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/peptide-coa-batch-number-matching.html · Updated: July 7, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **The core idea:** A COA only describes the specific batch it was run on. If the batch number on the certificate doesn't match the number on your vial, the report isn't about your product.

## Why batch matching matters

A clean report for a different lot tells you nothing about the vial in your hand. The number is the link between the paper and the product.

## Where to find the number

It's usually printed on the vial label and stated on the COA. Both should agree, and ideally verify on the lab's own portal.

## If they don't match

A mismatch means the certificate describes some other batch. Regard that as an unanswered question, not a pass.

Related: [COA reuse and theft](/articles/coa-reuse-and-theft.html) · [how to verify a COA](/articles/how-to-verify-a-coa.html).

# Vendor Communication Red Flags: The Tell Is How They Answer

_The communication red flags that reveal a peptide vendor, and the one question that exposes them. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/peptide-vendor-communication-red-flags.html · Updated: July 8, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer:** How a vendor answers a simple testing question tells you more than its entire homepage. Evasiveness is the tell.

## Green flags

A direct answer, a link to a COA that verifies on the lab's portal, the vendor named as the client, and a batch number that matches.

## Red flags

Deflection, screenshots instead of verifiable links, in-house-only numbers, or pressure to buy before you can check anything.

## The one question to ask

"Can you link the independent COA for this batch, verifiable on the lab's portal?" A confident vendor answers in one message. The rest reveal themselves.

Related: [vendor red-flags checklist](/articles/peptide-vendor-red-flags.html) · [how to read a COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html).

# Peptide Vendor Red Flags: The Checklist (2026)

_The red flags that separate a trustworthy peptide vendor from one selling on hype — no verifiable testing, captive labs, vague answers, and more. A practical checklist._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/peptide-vendor-red-flags.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **The fastest red-flag test:** if a vendor's only "proof" is a PDF they uploaded themselves, and the testing lab has no independent footprint, treat it as marketing — not evidence. Below are the seven flags that matter most, in order.

**🚩 1. Testing you can't independently verify.** A COA that only exists as a vendor-hosted PDF proves nothing — it could be reused, borrowed, or not your batch. You want results that resolve on the *lab's own* portal.
**🚩 2. A captive lab.** If the "independent" lab only ever tests for this one vendor, it's grading its own homework. Real independence means many competing vendors use the same lab.
**🚩 3. Suspicious timing.** The lab's website appeared days after the vendor's. A quick WHOIS lookup exposes this instantly.
**🚩 4. Weasel accreditation.** "ISO 17025-aligned" or "ISO-standard methods" ≠ accredited. Accreditation is a specific, checkable status.
**🚩 5. Vagueness under questioning.** Ask how they test. Open, specific answers are a good sign; "trust us" or topic-changes are not.
**🚩 6. No independent/blind testing anywhere.** The strongest signal is whether anyone has bought off-the-shelf and tested it blind. If the only testing is vendor-controlled, discount it.
**🚩 7. Pressure and hype over information.** Countdown timers and bold body claims, but no verifiable sourcing detail, is the profile of a vendor selling urgency instead of quality.

## Turn this into a 60-second check

You don't need to memorize all seven. Our free quiz runs exactly these flags against any vendor and scores it.

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)

Deeper dives: [how to read a COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html) · [how to spot a fake lab](/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html).

Related guides[How to read a peptide COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html) · [How to spot a fake testing lab](/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html) · [Buying peptides in Canada](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html) · [The Growth Guys discount code](/articles/growth-guys-discount-code.html)

# Do Peptide Orders Get Stopped at Canadian Customs?

_The practical sourcing question Canadians ask about research peptides and customs, and why buying from a domestic vendor sidesteps it. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/peptides-canada-customs.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer:** The practical question most Canadians ask isn't legal theory — it's whether a parcel clears smoothly. Buying from a vendor that ships *within* Canada is the simplest way to avoid the uncertainty that comes with international research-chemical shipments at the border.

## Why domestic shipping is the practical play

An order that never crosses an international border never faces the customs question in the first place. That's the entire appeal of a domestic vendor: fewer unknowns, faster delivery, and a clearer paper trail if anything goes wrong with the order.

## What people get wrong

Most buyers obsess over the border and ignore the thing that actually decides quality — whether the vendor's product is independently tested. A smooth delivery of an untested vial is not a win. Sourcing and testing are two separate questions, and the testing one matters more.

## How to vet a Canadian vendor before you order

- Confirm the vendor ships **domestically**, not drop-shipped from overseas.

- Insist the Certificate of Analysis **verifies on the lab's own portal**, with the vendor named.

- Prefer vendors that show **independent blind testing**, not just their own in-house numbers.

Related: [buying peptides in Canada](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html) · [how to read a COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html).

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)

# The Phantom "Principal Chemist": When a COA Signature Isn't Real

_Some peptide COAs are signed by a 'principal chemist' who has no verifiable existence — the same name on hundreds of documents, matching no real record. Here's how to check._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/phantom-coa-signatory.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **The tell:** a COA is signed by an official-sounding "Principal Chemist" — the same name appears on hundreds of documents across multiple vendors, yet that person matches **no verifiable professional record anywhere.** A real signatory has a footprint. A boilerplate signature line is just decoration.

## Why a signature line means so little

An impressive name and title at the bottom of a lab report *feels* like accountability. But a name is the easiest thing in the world to type. When the same "chemist" signs documents for several unrelated vendors, and you can't find that person in any professional registry, association, or institution, the signature is performing trust rather than providing it.

## How to check (2 minutes, free)

- **Search the exact name + "chemist"** and the lab name. A real professional usually has *some* footprint — a profile, a publication, an institutional page.

- **Look for the name across other vendors' COAs.** If the same signatory appears on documents for unrelated companies, it's likely a template, not a person accountable for your test.

- **Cross-check the lab itself.** A phantom signatory usually sits on top of a [captive or fake lab](/articles/what-is-a-captive-lab.html) — check the lab's independence and history too.

## What good looks like

A genuinely independent lab is an institution with staff, accreditation, and a public track record — its credibility doesn't rest on a single mysterious signature. The signature is the weakest possible trust signal; the lab's independence and a result that [verifies on its own portal](/articles/how-to-verify-a-coa.html) are what actually count.

[🧪 Run the free vendor check →](/vendor-quiz.html)

# What "Research Use Only" Actually Means on Peptides (Canada)

_Almost every peptide is labelled 'research use only / not for human consumption.' Here's what that label actually means, why vendors use it, and what it implies for buyers in Canada._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/research-use-only-meaning.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer:** "Research use only" (or "not for human consumption") is a **legal-status label**, not a quality claim. It signals the product is sold as a **laboratory research chemical** — not an approved drug or supplement — which is how vendors operate without Health Canada drug authorization. It tells you about the regulatory category, nothing about purity. This is general information, not legal or medical advice.

## Why vendors use the label

Most peptides have no Health Canada authorization (no DIN) for human use. Marketing them for human consumption would put them squarely in the regulated-drug system. By labelling them "research use only," vendors sell them in the grey-area "research chemical" category instead. The label is the legal line they operate behind — see [are peptides legal in Canada?](/articles/are-peptides-legal-in-canada.html)

## What the label does and doesn't tell you

- **It does say:** this is unapproved material sold outside the drug/supplement system, with no regulator checking it.

- **It does NOT say:** anything about purity, identity, or whether the testing is real. A "research use only" vial can be 99% pure or near-worthless — the label is silent on that.

## What it means for you as a buyer

Because no regulator vets these products, the label actually *raises* the importance of your own diligence. The quality signal isn't the label — it's independent, verifiable testing. Does the COA resolve on the [lab's own portal](/articles/how-to-verify-a-coa.html)? Is the lab [independent or captive](/articles/what-is-a-captive-lab.html)?

[🧪 Score a vendor's testing in 60 seconds →](/vendor-quiz.html)
Related guides[How to read a peptide COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html) · [How to spot a fake testing lab](/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html) · [Buying peptides in Canada](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html) · [The Growth Guys discount code](/articles/growth-guys-discount-code.html)

---
_General educational information, not legal or medical advice. Peptides discussed are not approved for human use in Canada. Posts may contain affiliate links._


---

# What Is a Captive Lab? (And Why It Matters for Peptides)

_A captive lab is a testing 'lab' that exists to test for one vendor only — grading its own homework. Here's how to recognize one and why independence is everything._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/what-is-a-captive-lab.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **A captive lab** is a testing "lab" that exists to test for **one vendor only** — often spun up by, or alongside, that vendor. Because it serves a single master, its "independent" results are really the vendor grading its own homework. Independence is the entire value of third-party testing, and a captive lab has none.

## Why it matters

The whole point of a third-party lab is that it has no stake in the result. A genuinely independent lab tests for *many competing* vendors — so it can't favour any one of them without losing the others. A captive lab has exactly one client to please, which removes the independence that made the testing meaningful in the first place.

## How to recognize one

- **One client.** Search the lab's name — if it only ever appears next to a single vendor, that's the tell.

- **Suspicious timing.** A quick WHOIS lookup shows the lab's domain registered around the same time as the vendor's — sometimes days apart.

- **No footprint.** No accreditation, no staff, no reviews, no presence outside that one vendor.

- **A "verify" portal that accepts anything.** Type a made-up report number; if it returns a valid-looking result, it's theatre.

More tells: [how to spot a fake testing lab](/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html).

## The contrast

A genuinely independent lab (used by hundreds of competing vendors, with a long track record and a real verification portal) is the opposite of captive. That independence — not a logo or a PDF — is what makes a result worth trusting.

[🧪 Check if a vendor's lab is independent →](/vendor-quiz.html)
Related guides[How to read a peptide COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html) · [How to spot a fake testing lab](/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html) · [Buying peptides in Canada](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html) · [The Growth Guys discount code](/articles/growth-guys-discount-code.html)

---

# What Is a Third-Party Lab, and Why Does It Matter?

_What a third-party testing lab is, how it differs from in-house testing, and why independence is the whole point. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/what-is-a-third-party-lab.html · Updated: July 8, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer:** A third-party lab is one with no financial stake in the outcome — it didn't make the product and doesn't profit if the result passes. That independence is the entire value.

## Third-party vs in-house

In-house testing is the seller grading its own homework. A third-party result comes from a separate party with nothing to gain from a clean number.

## Why independence is the point

The PDF isn't the value — the lack of incentive to fudge it is. A widely used independent lab like Janoshik is named as the client by the vendor, on the lab's own portal.

## What to ask for

- An independent result, not in-house.
- Verification on the lab's portal.
- The vendor named as the client.

Related: [Janoshik vs in-house](/articles/janoshik-vs-in-house-testing.html) · [spot a fake lab](/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html).

# Why Peptide Prices Vary So Much (and Why Cheapest Is a Warning)

_What drives the price differences between peptide vendors, and why the cheapest option is often a warning sign. Educational only._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/why-peptide-prices-vary.html · Updated: July 10, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer:** Price gaps between vendors usually reflect testing, sourcing, and shipping — not a deal. The cheapest vial is often the untested one.

## What you're actually paying for

Independent testing costs money. Domestic, reliable shipping costs money. A vendor that does neither can always undercut one that does.

## Why cheapest is a warning

When a price is far below everyone else, ask what got cut to get there. Usually it's the part you can't see on arrival — the testing.

## How to value-compare

Compare price against verifiable testing, not against price alone. Buying tested product once beats buying cheap product twice.

Related: [cost to test a vial](/articles/cost-to-test-a-peptide-vial.html) · [comparing Canadian vendors](/articles/how-to-compare-canadian-peptide-vendors.html).

# Why Do Two Peptide Vendors Have the Same COA? (2026)

_If two different vendors show the same Certificate of Analysis, here's what's actually going on — COA reuse, theft, and shared suppliers — and what it means for you._

Source: https://torontohealthsource.github.io/articles/why-two-vendors-same-coa.html · Updated: June 17, 2026 · TorontoHealth — educational only, not medical advice.


> **Short answer:** usually one of three things — **COA theft** (one vendor posted another's genuine document), **COA reuse** (a shared supplier's single test passed around), or both vendors genuinely buy from the **same source** and show its lab report. Only the third is benign, and you often can't tell which it is from the PDF alone.

## The three explanations

- **Theft.** A vendor downloads a competitor's (or their supplier's) real COA and presents it as their own. The document is authentic — it's just not *theirs*. Posting a real PDF only defeats forgery; it does nothing against theft.

- **Reuse.** A Chinese supplier tests one batch, and every reseller buying from that supplier shows the same report — even though their individual vials were never tested. One good test, thousands of untested vials sold against it.

- **Same genuine source.** Sometimes it's innocent: both vendors really do resell the same tested product. But you can't assume this.

## How to tell the difference

The PDF won't tell you — but these will:

- Does each vendor's COA **verify on the lab's own portal**, tied to *that* vendor as the client?

- Does the **client name** on the document actually match the vendor showing it?

- Is there any **independent blind testing** of either vendor's actual product?

The full method is in [how to read a peptide COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html).

[🧪 Check a vendor's testing in 60 seconds →](/vendor-quiz.html)

## The takeaway

Two vendors with the same COA is a yellow flag, not automatically fraud — but it means the document isn't doing the job people think it is. A COA proves a test happened on *some* sample. Whose sample, verified where, is the real question.

Related guides[How to read a peptide COA](/articles/how-to-read-a-peptide-coa.html) · [How to spot a fake testing lab](/articles/how-to-spot-a-fake-testing-lab.html) · [Buying peptides in Canada](/articles/buying-peptides-in-canada.html) · [The Growth Guys discount code](/articles/growth-guys-discount-code.html)
